Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd try to set aside time, but it never stuck. Most of my studying happened late at night when I remembered something was due, or when I felt guilty about not doing anything. I'd tell myself I'd study more consistently, but then days would pass and I hadn't touched anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Sometimes I'd have a burst of energy and study for hours, but then I'd go days without opening anything. Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to actually sit down and work. If nothing was due soon, I'd just drift and do other things.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline or seeing a notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I missed anything important, but I wouldn't actually do much once I was there. It felt more like checking a box than actually wanting to learn something.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic or natural. It always felt like a chore I had to force myself to do. Maybe early in the semester I tried to make it a habit, but that fell apart pretty quickly when things got stressful.
Q5: I actually found the lecture recordings helpful because I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things or just lie down and absorb the information. Reading long texts is harder for me, so having audio made a difference when I actually used it.
Q6: The discussion forums and reading materials I mostly ignored. I posted a few times because I had to, but I never really got into the discussions. The readings felt endless and I'd open them, skim a bit, then close them. I just couldn't focus on walls of text.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd actually do it. Activities that felt disconnected or like busy work just didn't seem worth the effort. When I'm already stretched thin, I need to see the point immediately or I'll skip it.
Q8: Optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. I know that sounds bad, but I was barely keeping up with the required work. Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend energy on.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone when I wasn't near my laptop. I also borrowed a friend's notes sometimes because I missed so many classes. That kind of studying wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd listen to downloaded lectures while on the bus or walking around. I'd also study with my peer support group sometimes, just talking through concepts out loud. None of that would register as me being online or active.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a recording once and then replay it multiple times offline. It was easier on my data and I could listen whenever. The LMS would only show I accessed it once, but I probably listened three or four times to some things.
Q12: Around midterms everything just crashed for me. I was stressed about money, stressed about falling behind, and I just stopped caring as much. It felt like I was too far behind to catch up anyway, so what was the point? That's when my attendance really dropped.
Q13: All of those things, honestly. I was tired all the time, confused about what I was supposed to be doing, and I had family stuff going on. When I logged into the LMS feeling like that, I'd just stare at the screen and not actually do anything productive.
Q14: So many times. I'd open a video or a reading and just zone out completely. My mind would be somewhere else, worrying about other things. I was technically accessing the material but nothing was going in. It was like my body was there but my brain wasn't.
Q15: I avoided the exam prep materials because they stressed me out. Looking at them reminded me how much I didn't know, so I'd put it off until the last minute. I knew it was important but facing it felt overwhelming, so I'd distract myself instead.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only reason I did anything. I'd scramble in the last few hours before something was due. It wasn't a healthy way to work, but without that pressure I just couldn't make myself engage. The urgency was what moved me.
Q17: I didn't really pay much attention to feedback, to be honest. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. Sometimes the feedback came too late to feel useful, and other times I just didn't want to face what I did wrong. It didn't change my behavior much.
Q18: The course had a lot of flexibility which sounds good but it was bad for me. I need structure and deadlines spread out, not big chunks of freedom. Too much freedom meant I'd put everything off. I needed someone or something pushing me along.
Q19: Having everything online made it easy to avoid. If I had to physically show up somewhere, I probably would have engaged more. The peer support helped a bit, but the overall design let me drift too easily. I needed more check-ins or something.
Q20: That could be someone like me who opens things but doesn't actually absorb them. You might click around a lot looking for something, getting confused, trying to figure out what to do. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just panic clicking.
Q21: That makes sense to me. Someone could study offline, use other resources, or just be really efficient. I did some studying that never showed up online. If I'd actually used that offline time better, maybe I could've done okay without much LMS activity.
Q22: The log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you understood anything. It can't tell if you were stressed, distracted, or just going through the motions. It misses all the emotional and mental stuff that affects learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking things. For me, I was rarely truly engaged this semester. I was just surviving, not really learning.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally or how disconnected I felt from the course. It wouldn't show the times I listened to recordings offline or talked things through with peers. It also wouldn't show that I actually wanted to do better but couldn't figure out how to make it happen.
